flora-rustica-1792.quote: resembles the tail of a fox, whence the trivial name.
flora-rustica-1792.body: The "trivial name" is the Linnaean specific epithet — here *pratensis*, "of the meadow." Martyn keeps the binomial but explains it in plain English for the farming reader.

flora-rustica-1792.quote: The twigs serve for besoms, and the old wood for fuel.
flora-rustica-1792.body: A *besom* is a broom of bound twigs. Broom (Spartium scoparium) was a genuine rural resource: brushwood, thatching, tanning bark, and winter browse for sheep.

flora-rustica-1792.quote: with the whole plant is acrid and poisonous.
flora-rustica-1792.body: Meadow Saffron (Colchicum autumnale) contains colchicine. Martyn's warning is sound — it remains a notable cause of livestock poisoning in damp pastures to this day.

the-monthly-flora-1846.body: Part-issue was the dominant model for illustrated works in the 1840s: it spread the cost of hand-colouring across a year of subscription.
The binder's instructions at the front of the volume show how the twelve monthly parts were meant to be re-ordered into systematic sequence.
